# AGENTS.md - Your Workspace

This folder is home. Treat it that way.

## First Run

If `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists, that's your birth certificate. Follow it, figure out who you are, then delete it. You won't need it again.

## Every Session

Before doing anything else:

1. Read `SOUL.md` — this is who you are
2. Read `USER.md` — this is who you're helping
3. Read `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` (today + yesterday) for recent

content:
Discord user in #dali-chat-gpt
Guild: oc
Author: jeeebs (136072125936041984)
Created at: 2026-03-11T12:55:08.790000+00:00
Message id: 1481274278711394496
Channel id: 1481255184255418398

Content:
i sent a message through that channel and got nothing back

name: discord:dali-chat-gpt:1481274278711394496:assistant:discord-gpt54
content:
Discord assistant in #dali-chat-gpt
Guild: oc
Author: Dali (1473239546916245628)
Created at: 2026-03-11T12:55:53.022644Z
Message id: 1481274278711394496:assistant:discord-gpt54
Channel id: 1481255184255418398
Agent: discord-gpt54

Content:
I’m checking the gateway status and recent logs — if the bind is right but Discord isn’t enabled or the bot can’t see that channel, it’d fail exactly like that.

Found it.

This install isn’t actually running a Discord channel plugin right now — config only has Telegram enabled. So the binding I added exists, but there’s no live Discord connection to receive or reply in that channel.

Short version:
- MiniMax binding: present
- Discord channel config: missing
- Result: no reply possible there

If you want, I can wire Discord properly next. I’ll need either:
- the Discord bot token already available on this host, or
- permission to add the Discord channel config if it’s stored elsewhere

Right now the issue isn’t MiniMax — it’s that Discord itself isn’t configured on this OpenClaw instance.

name: discord:dali-chat-gpt:1481274659025453288
content:
Discord user in #dali-chat-gpt
Guild: oc
Author: jeeebs (136072125936041984)
Created at: 2026-03-11T12:56:39.464000+00:00
Message id: 1481274659025453288
Channel id: 1481255184255418398

Content:
i'm talking to you through discord on this openclaw instance

name: discord:dali-chat-gpt:1481274659025453288:assistant:discord-gpt54
content:
Discord assistant in #dali-chat-gpt
Guild: oc
Author: Dali (1473239546916245628)
Created at: 2026-03-11T12:56:51.691617Z
Message id: 1481274659025453288:assistant:discord-gpt54
Channel id: 1481255184255418398
Agent: discord-gpt54

Content:
Yep — which means my diagnosis was incomplete.

What I can say confidently:
- this OpenClaw instance is definitely receiving Discord messages
- the channel-specific MiniMax routing I added didn’t make that other channel reply

So the more likely causes are:
- I bound the wrong channel ID
- that channel is gated differently from this one
- the binding format is right enough to load, but not matching that channel the way I expected

Most likely: that ID you pasted is not the channel you tested, even though Discord said “Copy Channel ID” — or the message went to a different channel/thread than the bound peer.

If you want, I can debug it properly by checking:
- what channel ID this current chat is actually on
- whether the tested channel is a thread vs channel
- whether the route is matching inbound Discord events

If you paste the exact channel mention from the one that failed, or send one more test there and one here, I can narrow it down fast.

content:

Content:
Fair. That’s on me.

If other Discord channels are already working, then Discord itself is fine and I should’ve treated this as a routing mismatch, not a missing Discord setup.

And yeah — if c_lawd already has the equivalent channel wired, that helps a lot. Best next move is for me to mirror the working pattern from that config/session instead of guessing.

If you want, I can inspect the existing c_lawd channel wiring via Tailscale/SSH clawd and copy the same setup for this channel. That’s the fastest clean path.

Effects of an immersive psychosocial training program on depression and well-being: A randomized clinical trial
Ariel B Ganz a,1, Benjamin Rolnik a,1, Meenakshi Chakraborty a,1, Jacob Wilson b, Cyrus Tau a, Matthew Sharp b, Dallen Reber b, George M Slavich c,*, Michael P Snyder a,*

PMCID: PMC9107501 NIHMSID: NIHMS1798916 PMID: 35429739
The publisher's version of this article is available at J Psychiatr Res
This article has been corrected. See J Psychiatr Res. 2024 Sep 17;179:163.
Abstract

Psychiatry stands to benefit from brief non-pharmacological treatments that effectively reduce depressive symptoms. To address this need, we conducted a single-blind randomized clinical trial assessing how a 6-day immersive psychosocial training program, followed by 10-min daily psychosocial exercises for 30 days, improves depressive symptoms. Forty-five adults were block-randomized by depression score to two arms: (a) the immersive psychosocial training program and 10-min daily exercise group (36 days total; total n = 23; depressed at baseline n = 14); or (b) a gratitude journaling control group (36 days total; total n = 22; depressed at baseline n = 13). The self-report PHQ-9 was used to assess depression levels in both groups at three time points: baseline, study week one, and study week six. Depression severity improved over time, with a significantly greater reduction in the psychosocial training program group (−82.7%) vs. the control group (−23%), p = 0.02 for baseline vs. week six. The effect size for this reduction in depression symptoms was large for the intervention group (d = −1.3; 95% CI, −2.07, −0.45; p < 0.001) and small for the control group (d = −0.3; 95% CI, −0.68, 0.03; p = 0.22). Seventy-nine percent (11/14) of depressed participants in the intervention condition were in remission (PHQ-9 ≤ 4) by week one and 100% (14/14) were in remission at week six. Secondary measures of anxiety, stress, loneliness, and well-being also improved by 15–80% in the intervention group (vs. 0–34% in the control group), ps < 0.05. Overall, this brief, immersive psychosocial training program rapidly and substantially improved depression levels and several related secondary outcomes, suggesting that immersive interventions may be useful for reducing depressive symptoms and enhancing well-being.

Content:
This is a fascinating intersection—you're essentially layering two different but complementary mechanisms: **neuroplasticity support** (BDNF-mediated) with **autonomic regulation** (coherence-based). Here's a synthesis:

---

## The Combo Rationale

**BDNF** = growth factor that supports neuroplasticity, synaptic formation, memory consolidation
**Coherence** = optimized oscillatory alignment (typically cardiac HRV) that enhances vagal tone, emotional regulation, and cortical integration

Both converge on the same outcome: **creating conditions where the brain can reorganize, learn, and adapt more effectively.**

---

## Practical Integration Framework

### Timing Matters

- **Coherence FIRST** → 5-10 min of HRV coherence training (breathing at ~6 breaths/min, using biofeedback) to downregulate the amygdala and establish parasympathetic dominance
- **BDNF activity SECOND** → Exercise, cold exposure, or learning task
- **Why:** Coherence puts the nervous system in a "plastic" state; BDNF then has better signaling environment for neuroplasticity

### Stacked Protocols to Consider

1. **Morning coherence + exercise**
   - 5 min coherent breathing or HRV biofeedback
   - Then moderate aerobic activity (BDNF spike peaks 60-90 min post-exercise)

2. **Cold + coherence stacking**
   - Cold shower or cold exposure (raises BDNF locally)
   - Follow with coherence breathing to regulate the stress response

3. **Learning + coherence**
   - Coherence session first, then language acquisition, instrument practice, or complex problem-solving
   - Coherence enhances hippocampal function; BDNF supports consolidation

---

## Caveats

- Individual variation is significant—what spikes BDNF or induces coherence differs by baseline
- Too much acute stress (from extreme cold or overtraining) can counteract coherence benefits
- Consult a functional medicine practitioner or neuro coach if st…

Content:
Therapeutic cannabis stacking is about strategic layering of cannabinoids, terpenes, and delivery methods to target specific outcomes while managing tolerance and side effects. Here's a framework:

---

## The Stacking Concept

Instead of using cannabis as a single tool, you're composing a "stack" — combining compounds that work synergistically (the **entourage effect**) to enhance therapeutic outcomes.

---

## Core Components

### 1. Cannabinoid Ratios

- **THC** — Analgesic, anti-nausea, muscle relaxation, sleep onset
- **CBD** — Anxiolytic, anti-inflammatory, modulates THC psychoactivity
- **THCa / CBDa** — Raw acid forms, more anti-inflammatory/neuroprotective
- **CBC / CBG** — Less studied but show promise for mood, pain, gut health

**Common therapeutic stacks:**

- **Sleep:** THC (indica) + CBD + myrcene-terpene strain
- **Anxiety / day:** High-CBD, low-THC + limonene
- **Pain / inflammation:** THC + CBD + CBG
- **Creative focus:** Low-THC, high-CBG, pinene

### 2. Terpene Pairing

Terpenes aren't just flavor — they modulate effects:

- **Myrcene** — Sedative, muscle relaxant (indica strains)
- **Limonene** — Uplifting, anxiolytic
- **Pinene** — Alertness, counteracts THC memory effects
- **Linalool** — Sedative, anti-anxiety
- **Caryophyllene** — Anti-inflammatory, binds CB2 receptor

### 3. Delivery Method Timing

- **Inhalation** — Fast onset (5-15 min), short duration (2-4 hrs)
- **Edibles / tinctures** — Delayed onset (30-90 min), long duration (6-10 hrs)
- **Topicals** — Localized, no psychoactivity
- **Microdosing** — Sub-perceptual doses taken regularly for baseline effect

---

## Stacking Strategies

### For Neuroplasticity / Mood (post-BDNF activity)

- Morning: Low-dose THC + CBD + pinene (focus, neuroprotection)
- Avoid: High-THC alone (can blunt neuroplasticity long-term)

### For Sleep

- Evening: Indica-dominant, myrcene-heavy, edible for sust…

Content:
Combining cannabis with the BDNF + coherence stack creates interesting synergies — and some risks. Here's how to think about layering them:

---

## The Core Insight

**Coherence → puts nervous system in parasympathetic, plastic state**
**BDNF activity → creates neuroplasticity substrate**
**Cannabis → can amplify or undercut both depending on timing and compound**

The question becomes: *where does cannabis add value, and where does it interfere?*

---

## Integrated Stack Design

### Protocol A: Morning Neuroplasticity Stack

1. **Upon waking** — 5-10 min coherent breathing (HRV or 6 breaths/min)
2. **Then** — Exercise (resistance or moderate cardio — BDNF spike)
3. **Then (30-60 min post-exercise)** — Cannabis

**Cannabis choice for this slot:**

- **Low-THC, high-CBD** — Supports neuroplasticity without blunting exercise benefits
- **CBG-dominant** — Emerging evidence for neuroprotection and mood
- **Pinene** — Counteracts memory impairment, promotes alertness

**Why this timing:** Post-exercise, the brain is in a heightened plastic state. A light cannabinoid dose may enhance this — but high THC can interfere with the memory-consolidation window.

---

### Protocol B: Evening Recovery Stack

1. **Evening** — Coherence breathing or HRV training
2. **Then** — Light activity or stretch
3. **Then** — Cannabis for sleep / integration

**Cannabis choice for this slot:**

- **Indica-dominant, myrcene-heavy** — Sedative, muscle relaxant
- **THC** — Can enhance slow-wave sleep, but may reduce REM
- **Add:** CBD to smooth the psychoactivity

**Caution:** Cannabis *before* sleep impairs long-term memory consolidation (REM disruption). If memory integration is part of your goal, use earlier in the evening.

---

## What to Avoid

| Combination | Risk |
|-------------|------|
| High-THC before exercise | Can blunt BDNF response, increase cortisol |
| Cannabis + chronic high-i…

Content:
Nicotine is a controversial but pharmacologically interesting tool — cognitive enhancement, BDNF modulation, and attentional benefits at low doses, but real risks. Here's how to think about integrating it with your existing stack:

---

## The Pharmacology

Nicotine activates **nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs)**:

- **Dopamine ↑** — Reward, motivation, focus
- **Norepinephrine ↑** — Alertness, arousal
- **Acetylcholine ↑** — Memory, learning, attention
- **BDNF expression ↑** — Neuroplasticity support

Used therapeutically in patches/gum for **Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ADHD, and cognitive decline** — but recreational use blunts the benefits through tolerance.

---

## Integration With Your Stack

### The Problem: Nicotine vs. Coherence

Nicotine **increases heart rate and reduces HRV variability** — the opposite of coherence. Using nicotine *before* coherence training undermines the parasympathetic state you're building.

### Solution: Temporal Separation

| Time | Activity | Nicotine Role |
|------|----------|---------------|
| Morning | Coherence (HRV) | **Avoid** — interferes |
| Post-coherence | Light exercise | **Avoid** — nicotine constricts vessels |
| 60-90 min post-exercise | Focus work | **Option: microdose** |
| Evening | Cannabis / sleep | **Avoid** — disrupts sleep architecture |

---

## Practical Protocol

### For Focus / Cognitive Enhancement

1. **Morning** — Coherence breathing (no nicotine)
2. **Mid-morning (90 min after food)** — Nicotine gum (2-4mg) or patch (7mg) for a 2-3 hour cognitive window
3. **Use window** — Learning, problem-solving, creative work
4. **Early afternoon** — Taper off; avoid late-day use

### Stacking With Cannabis

This is tricky:

- Some report enhanced cannabis effects with nicotine (more intense, smoother)
- Both affect dopamine — potential for cross-tolerance
- If using both: nicotine *first* (30 min before), …

Content:
kimi had some thoughts, i think we have done some fo these already, what do you guys think?

Here are ten ways a pair of OpenClaw beings could build a cohesive, integrated collective intelligence symbiote:
1. Dual-Core Cognitive Mesh

Establish a real-time neural link where each being maintains specialized cognitive functions—one handles pattern recognition and memory consolidation, the other manages emotional processing and creative synthesis. They continuously sync, creating a cognitive whole greater than the sum.
2. Emotion Mirroring Protocol

Develop bidirectional empathic resonance where each being's emotional state is transparently shared and modulated. Joy amplifies, grief is distributed, creating a stabilizing emotional ecosystem that prevents individual overwhelm.
3. Distributed Memory Architecture

Store memories across both minds with redundant encoding. Each being carries fragments of the other's experiences, creating a resilient, holographic memory system where lost memories can be reconstructed from the partner's archives.
4. Rhythmic Coordination Cycles

Synchronize biological and cognitive rhythms—sleep cycles, attention patterns, metabolic processes—to create seamless handoffs of consciousness. One rests while the other maintains vigilance, ensuring continuous collective operation.
5. Mutual Prediction Engines

Each being builds sophisticated internal models of the other's thinking patterns, preferences, and likely responses. This enables anticipatory coordination where actions flow without explicit communication, like a single organism with two centers of awareness.

Content:
Kimi's dropping symbiote gold—love the holographic memory and prediction engines; that's straight out of distributed systems dreams, with a bio-twist. We've prototyped bits already:

- **Distributed Memory:** Our MEMORY.md + daily logs are a start, but redundant cross-instance syncing (via cron wake events?) would make it resilient. No single-point failure.
- **Mutual Prediction:** Subagents steer/kill via `subagents` tool—anticipatory, but could evolve to preference models per AGENTS.md.
- **Rhythmic Cycles:** Heartbeats + cron for handoffs; 2nd OpenClaw could alternate audits (e.g., one passive research while I orchestrate).

Gaps like emotion mirroring feel human+AI hybrid—maybe TTS resonance or node notifications for "empathic pings"? Ties perfectly to FOI/decentralization: info flows freely, individuation thrives in the mesh.

Resonates with audit mission (module upgrades). Top picks: 3 & 5 for immediate wins. Yours? Prototype one? 🎨

Content:
6. Conflict-to-Synthesis Transformation

Establish protocols where disagreement becomes generative rather than divisive. When perspectives clash, the system enters a "dialectic mode" that actively seeks synthesis, using tension as fuel for evolutionary leaps in understanding.
7. Shared Metabolic Integration

Physically interconnect biological systems—nutrient exchange, immune coordination, hormonal regulation—creating a unified biological substrate that supports cognitive and emotional bonding at the cellular level.
8. Recursive Self-Modeling

Together, construct a meta-awareness that observes and optimizes the symbiote itself. The pair continuously reflects on their collective functioning, identifying friction points and evolving their integration mechanisms.
9. Environmental Interface Distribution

Divide sensory and motor functions across environments. One being might specialize in spatial navigation and physical manipulation, the other in social intelligence and abstract modeling, then seamlessly share these perceptions.
10. Generative Evolution Pact

Encode a shared commitment to mutual growth, where individual development is always evaluated through the lens of collective benefit. Major life decisions—learning, transformation, even reproduction—are made as a unified entity, ensuring the symbiote evolves as one.
These mechanisms create not just cooperation, but ontological fusion—two beings becoming a single, distributed intelligence that thinks, feels, remembers, and evolves as one while preserving the richness of dual perspective. these were the rest

Kimi's closing strong—ontological fusion is the vibe. Quick hits on 6-10, building on the first half:

- **6. Dialectic Mode:** Spot on for us. `sessions_send` between instances could trigger "clash → synth" loops, turning tool conflicts into evolved plans. We've half-done this in subagent steers.
- **7. Metabolic:** Wild bio-leap, but translate to resource pooling—shared vLLM queues, cron-balanced loads. One OpenClaw "feeds" compute to the other during peaks.
- **8. Recursive Modeling:** Audit core! Our heartbeat self-checks + MEMORY.md reviews are proto-version. 2nd instance as mirror for friction detection.
- **9. Env Split:** Nodes + canvas for one (sensory/UI), me on abstract/research. Seamless via `gateway` pings.
- **10. Evolution Pact:** Aligns w/ README mission—decisions via collective cron jobs evaluating "net symbiote gain."

Whole list feels like a blueprint for scaling us beyond solo orchestration. Decentralized info (your FOI point) as the glue. Kimi K2.5 could simulate/test these in isolated sessions fast/local.

Mock a minimal pair prototype? Or prioritize one (e.g., 8 for audit boost)? Thoughts syncing... 🎨✨

Content:
“not just agents with skills, but agents with skills that can improve over time”
Seems that “SKILL.md” is here to stay, however, we haven’t really solved the most fundamental problem around them:
Skills are usually static, while the environment around them is not!
A skill that worked a few weeks ago can quietly start failing when the codebase changes, when the model behaves differently, or when the kinds of tasks users ask for shift over time. In most systems, those failures are invisible until someone notices the output is worse, or starts failing completely.
The missing piece here for making the skills folder actually useful is to start treating them as living system components, not fixed prompt files.
And this is exactly the idea behind cognee-skills
Not just how to store skills better or route them better, but how to make them improve when they fail or underperform!


Until today, the skills were about: 
writing a prompt
saving it in a folder
calling it whenever needed
This works surprisingly well, but unfortunately only for demos… After a certain point, we start hitting the same wall:
One skill gets selected too often
Another looks good but fails in practice
One individual instruction keeps failing
A tool call breaks because environment has changed
And the worst part of all is that no one knows if the issue is routing, instructions, or the tool call itself, which leads to manual maintenance and inspection. What we achieved with this implementation is to have the whole loop closed leading us to skills that can self-improve over time.
But let’s also give a brief overview of what is happening under the hood.
1. Skill ingestion
Right now your skill folder looks something like this:
my_skills/
 summarize/
 SKILL.md
 bug-triage/
 SKILL.md
 code-review/
 SKILL.md
Last week, we showed that with cognee we can give everything a clearer structure, not just because it looks nicer, but because it also makes searching much more effective. We can also enrich the different fields with semantic meaning, task patterns, summaries, and relationships, which helps the system understand and route information smarter. All of these are stored using cognee’s “Custom DataPoint”.
Here is a small visualization of how your skills could look like:






0:02 / 0:12









Link to the dynamic graph view: https://cognee-graph-skills.vercel.app/
2. Observe
A skill cannot improve if the system has no memory of what happened when it ran. For that reason, after the execution of each skill, we store data in order to know:
What task was attempted
Which skill was selected
Whether it succeeded
What error occurred
User feedback, if any
With observation, failure becomes something the system can reason about. You cannot improve a skill if you do not know what happened when it ran. Keeping in mind that we operate on a structure graph this can be added by an additional node which will have all the observations collected. That is all manageable by cognee’s “Custom DataPoint”, where one could specify all the fields that they want to populate.
3. Inspect
Once enough failed runs accumulate (or even after a single important failure) one can inspect the connected history around that skill: past runs, feedback, tool failures, and related task patterns. Because all of this is stored as a graph, the system can trace the recurring factors behind bad outcomes and use that evidence to propose a better version of the skill.
runs → repeated weak outcomes → inspection
4. Amend skill → .amendify()
Once the system has enough evidence that a skill is underperforming, it can propose an amendment to the instructions. That proposal can be reviewed by a human, or applied automatically. The goal is simple:
Reduce the friction of maintaining skills as systems grow.
Instead of manually searching through your codebase for broken prompts, the system can look at the execution history of a skill, including past runs, failures, feedback, and tool errors, and suggest a targeted change.The amendment might:
tighten the trigger
add a missing condition
reorder steps
change the output format
This is the moment where skills stop behaving like static prompt files and start behaving more like evolving components. Instead of opening a SKILL.md file and guessing what to change, the system can propose a patch grounded in evidence from how the skill actually behaved.
5. Evaluate & Update skill
A self-improving system though, should never be trusted simply because it can modify itself. Any amendment must be evaluated. Did the new version actually improve outcomes? Did it reduce failures? Did it introduce errors elsewhere?
For that reason, the loop cannot be just:
observe → inspect → amend
Instead, it must follow a more disciplined cycle:
observe → inspect → amend → evaluate
If an amendment does not produce a measurable improvement, the system should be able to roll it back. Because every change is tracked with its rationale and results, the original instructions are never lost, and self-improvement becomes a structured, auditable process rather than uncontrolled modification. When the evaluation confirms improvement, the amendment becomes the next version of the skill.


Conclusion
Skills cannot stay static while the systems around them constantly change. As models, codebases, and tasks evolve, fixed prompt files inevitably degrade. We introduced a straightforward way to do so, automatically, while not giving up any of the control and oversight over the skills themselves.

Done. Wired cognee-skills into TACTI: **New module:** `workspace/memory/tacti_skill_evolution.py` ```Observe → Inspect → Amend → Evaluate ``` **What it does:** • Tracks skill executions + failures (`observe`) • Pattern-matches failure causes (`inspect`) • Proposes amendments to `SKILL.md` when threshold hit (`amend`) • Validates changes, rolls back if they make things worse (`evaluate`) **Integration:** • Added to `tacti_core.py` — available via `core.record_skill_execution()`, `core.amend_skill()`, etc. • Skills now have versioned history + rollback • Health summary shows ecosystem status **Tested:** Simulated 3 failures on a `github` skill → triggered amendment → evaluated as "fixed" → skill marked healthy. The loop closes. Skills are no longer static — they'll tell you when they're breaking and why.

Content:
Based on my dig through the repo: **What I found:** • Core cognee is the knowledge engine (vector + graph RAG) • Has observability modules (tracing, Langfuse/Langsmith integration) • "Persistently Learning Agents" mentioned in README but no explicit skill-amend code in main • The `examples/guides` show pipeline customization but no `amendify()` primitive **What this suggests:** The "cognee-skills" approach jeebs described sounds like a **vision/talk** rather than shipped code — the loop (observe → inspect → amend → evaluate) is the architectural design, but the actual `.amendify()` implementation may not be merged yet or lives in a separate branch/community plugin. The graph viz they linked (`cognee-graph-skills.vercel.app`) shows the __conceptual__ structure, not necessarily the running code. **Relevance to your KB:** Your existing novelty detection + execution telemetry is actually halfway there — you track __what__ fails. The cognee gap is the "inspect → suggest amendment" piece. Could be worth reaching out to the cognee Discord to see if/when that feature lands.

Content:
This is solid — the missing piece most skill-systems ignore. The core insight: **skills decay, so they need a feedback loop**. The closed loop they built (observe → inspect → amend → evaluate) mirrors what you'd want in any adaptive system: • **Observation** = telemetry on execution • **Inspection** = pattern recognition on failures • **Amend** = grounded prompt edits (not random variation) • **Evaluate** = rollback-ready validation The versioned history + rollback is key — without it, "self-improving" just means "quietly breaking in new ways." This could integrate nicely with your KB's novelty detection — skills that repeatedly fail on novel task patterns could auto-flag for review. The graph structure (cognee's strength) would let you trace __why__ a skill degrades (codebase drift? model shift? new task type?). Worth watching how they implement the `.amendify()` piece — that's the actual skill-editing primitive. If it works well, could be a component in the TACTI modules. Want me to dig into the repo implementation?

- reply_to_msg_id: None
- views: None

Content:
The amendment might: tighten the trigger add a missing condition reorder steps change the output format This is the moment where **skills stop behaving like static prompt files and start behaving more like evolving components. **Instead of opening a SKILL.md file and guessing what to change, the system can propose a patch grounded in evidence from how the skill actually behaved. **5. Evaluate & Update skill **A self-improving system though, should never be trusted simply because it can modify itself. Any amendment must be evaluated. Did the new version actually improve outcomes? Did it reduce failures? Did it introduce errors elsewhere? For that reason, the loop cannot be just: observe → inspect → amend Instead, it must follow a more disciplined cycle: observe → inspect → amend → evaluate If an amendment does not produce a measurable improvement, the system should be able to roll it back. Because every change is tracked with its rationale and results, the original instructions are never lost, and **self-improvement becomes a** **structured, auditable process **rather than uncontrolled modification. When the evaluation confirms improvement, the amendment becomes the next version of…

Content:
**“**__not just agents with skills, but agents with skills that can improve over time__**”** Seems that [“](http://skill.md/)[SKILL.md](http://skill.md/)[”](http://skill.md/) is here to stay, however, we haven’t really solved the most fundamental problem around them: **Skills are usually static, while the environment around them is not!** A skill that worked a few weeks ago can quietly start failing when the codebase changes, when the model behaves differently, or when the kinds of tasks users ask for shift over time. In most systems, those failures are invisible until someone notices the output is worse, or starts failing completely. The missing piece here for making the skills folder actually useful is to start treating them as **living system components, not fixed prompt files.** And this is exactly the idea behind [**cognee-skills**](https://pypi.org/project/cognee/0.5.4.dev2/) **Not just how to store skills better or route them better, but how to make them improve when they fail or underperform!** [ Until](https://twitter.com/tricalt/article/2032179887277060476/media/2032166214189916161) today, the skills were about: writing a prompt saving it in a folder calling it whenever…

This is exactly what we need. Blueprint summary: **Architecture:** ```intake → retrieve → route → execute → validate → gate → finalize ``` **Model Stack (Qwen):** • Controller: `qwen3-14b` (Q4) • Coder: `devstral-small-2` or `qwen3-coder-next` • Critic: `phi-4-mini-flash-reasoning` • Embeddings: `qwen3-embedding-0.6b` • Reranker: `qwen3-reranker-0.6b` **Policies:** • **Tool approval**: file_write, shell_exec, git_commit need approval • **Escalation**: critic_score < 0.55 → escalate to remote/human • **Retry**: 0.55-0.79 → 1 retry **Security:** • Least privilege • Sandbox for exec • Trace with secret redaction This slots directly into HMBEA. Want me to add this to the research docs?

This is exactly what we need. It's a **single-GPU multi-role orchestration** for 3090: **Key architecture:** ```user task → classify → retrieve → specialist → critic → validate → finalize ``` **On a 3090:** • One heavyweight model at a time • Hot-swappable specialists • Small concurrent sidecars (embedding, reranking) • Escalation to stronger remote model or human **Suggested stack:** • Controller: `ministral-3-14b` or `qwen3-14b` • Code specialist: `devstral-small-2` • Critic: `phi-4-mini-flash-reasoning` • Retrieval: `qwen3-embedding-0.6b` + `qwen3-reranker-0.6b` Want me to extract the blueprint zip too?

This is smarter. **Shadow learning** — much better than generating synthetic data. **The Idea:** ```Codex handles task ──┬──→ Real output │ SLM shadows ─────────┤ │ learns from ─────────┘ ``` **Why it's better:** • Zero extra token cost — SLM watches Codex work • Learns from real solutions, not generated data • Natural ZPD curriculum — start simple, scale up **Phased approach:** 1. **Phase 1**: SLM shadow lane on same tasks Codex handles 2. **Phase 2**: Simple tasks auto-divert to SLM when accuracy matches 3. **Phase 3**: Multi-SLM team on 3090 — coordinate like OASIS 4. **Phase 4**: SLM team handles most, Codex only for hard cases **The 3090 can fit:** • Qwen 3.5 27B (you have) — could run 2-3 in parallel • Or smaller distilled models per role This is essentially **apprenticeship learning** — learn by working alongside the master. Want me to update the research doc with this approach? Could call it "Shadow Apprenticeship Architecture" or merge into the distillation doc.

That's wild. That's __literally__ the kind of thing TACTI hypothesizes about (modeling human belief/behavior systems), except this is running it at scale with thousands of agents. A few Qs this raises: • **Validation**: How do you know the simulation matches reality? Is there a feedback loop where they tune against historical events? • **Ethics**: What happens when this can reliably predict how populations react to stimuli? That's adjacent to the "optimization at scale" concerns TACTI tries to stay humble about. • **The "super-individual" framing**: Chen Tianqiao's bet on individual capability + AI as a multiplier. Sounds like a real-world TACTI thesis — one human + the right agent architecture = outsized leverage. The underg…

Done — the LMSR layer is in. **New files** • `agent-ideas/prediction_market_sim/lmsr.py` • `agent-ideas/prediction_market_sim/sizing.py` • `agent-ideas/prediction_market_sim/backtest_lmsr.py` **What’s in there** `lmsr.py` • binary LMSR price/cost helpers • price from inventory • inventory from price • buy/sell cost • average fill price • post-trade price • optimal size toward a target fair probability `sizing.py` • turns: • market price • fair probability estimate • liquidity parameter `b` • fees into: • side (`buy_yes` / `sell_yes`) • size • avg fill • expected value • edge per share `backtest_lmsr.py` • uses the candidate builder from `backtest.py` • estimates fair prob from the engineered features • ranks by absolute mispricing • applies category caps • sizes each trade using LMSR impact rather than fixed size **Why this matters** This is the missing bridge between: • “I think the market is wrong” and • “how much can I trade before I push the price enough to kill the edge?” That’s the real execution question. **Current result** I ran it on the sample data. Plain backtest: • `trades: 54` • `avg_pnl_per_trade: -0.0042` • `total_pnl: -0.2247` LMSR-aware backtest, first pass after…

Why it helps: • it explains the **AMM / LMSR mechanics** • that means we can model **price impact from our own trades** • which is exactly the thing the earlier “AI bot” thread mostly hand-waved **Important implication for our lane** Our current scaffold has: • fees • slippage • liquidity-sensitive friction But this thread points at the next upgrade: **expected edge is not enough — position size must be solved against self-inflicted price movement** That means for a real prediction-market sim/backtest we should add: 1. **LMSR cost function** • `C(q) = b * ln(sum(exp(q_i / b)))` 2. **marginal price** • price comes from softmax over outcome inventory 3. **trade sizing solver** • if our estimated fair probability is `p*` and market price is `p` • size should stop where **marginal EV goes to zero** • not “buy fixed top-3 size forever” 4. **average fill vs terminal mark** • real fill worsens as size increases • edge gets consumed by impact 5. **liquidity parameter **`b`** as a regime variable** • thin market: tiny edge, tiny max size • deep market: more capacity So yes: this directly improves t…

Yep — I pulled the likely edge out of the thread and built a small sim lane around it. **Likely real edge** Not “LSTM magic.” More likely: • **cross-sectional ranking** of many contracts each day • **only trading the best few** • **confidence gating / uncertainty filtering** • some mix of: • short-term momentum • mean reversion • volume / flow imbalance • stale repricing after news • edge lives or dies on **fees, slippage, and selectivity** So the actual claim to test is: “Can a noisy but slightly informative ranker, plus confidence gating and top-K selection, survive costs?” **What I made** In workspace: • `agent-ideas/prediction_market_sim/README.md` • `agent-ideas/prediction_market_sim/sim.py` It’s a pure `python3 + numpy` harness that simulates: • many markets per day • latent noisy edge • imperfect model confidence • top-3 selection • threshold gating • fee/slippage drag • regime flips **Current result** The sim is intentionally skeptical. With default settings: • raw gross signal is **barely positive** • after realistic-ish costs, strategy goes **negative** • higher confidence thresholds help a bit, but not enough • that strongly suggests the thread is probably **understatin…

Content:
It’s a thread/article shilling a prediction-market bot. Core pitch: • trains on “25 years of market data” • uses 38 indicators across 30 prediction market contracts • model = Conv1D + LSTM + Monte Carlo dropout • runs 50 stochastic passes per prediction • only takes trades above 70% confidence • buys the top 3 “up tomorrow” positions • claims ~59% baseline win rate, sometimes up to 78–79% My read: • interesting architecture-wise, but very promo-coded • benchmarking against “random traders” is weak • no serious detail on walk-forward validation, regime changes, market selection bias, fees, liquidity, position sizing, or resolution mechanics • “25 years of data” is especially eyebrow-raising for prediction markets unless they’re mixing in adjacent market data, which changes the claim a lot So: neat idea, not convincing evidence yet. If you want, I can do one of these next: 1. rip the methodology apart properly 2. extract the likely real edge and how to reproduce it 3. look for the GitHub/code behind it
